content.text: Turn detection is the mechanism that decides when a user has finished speaking and the agent should begin responding. In a voice interface, every millisecond of unnecessary silence adds friction, but interrupting a user mid-sentence destroys trust. Getting this boundary right — automatically, in real time, across accents, noise levels, and languages — is one of the most consequential engineering choices in a voice agent.

Turn detection (also called end-of-speech detection or speech endpointing) is the process of segmenting a continuous audio stream into discrete conversational turns. A turn is one complete utterance from the user — everything from the first voiced syllable to the last, including natural mid-sentence pauses.

The core component is a Voice Activity Detector (VAD), which classifies each short audio frame as speech or non-speech. The turn detection layer wraps the VAD in a state machine: it waits for a speech onset, buffers audio until a sustained silence is detected, then commits the buffered utterance as a completed turn. Three implementation strategies exist:
— computes RMS amplitude or zero-crossing rate per frame and compares to a fixed threshold. Simple, zero-dependency, but sensitive to background noise and microphone gain.
— a small neural model (e.g., Silero VAD at 1.8 MB, WebRTC VAD using GMMs) assigns a speech probability score per frame. More robust to noise and variable microphone gain.

— sends the ongoing transcription to an LLM and asks whether the utterance is semantically complete. Catches cases where a speaker pauses mid-thought but hasn't finished speaking. High accuracy, but adds 200–500 ms of extra latency.

Voice conversations rely on a tight feedback loop. If the turn boundary is detected too early, the agent interrupts the user — a failure mode that causes users to feel unheard and quickly abandon the product. If the boundary is detected too late, users experience dead air and assume the agent is broken. Research on conversational timing shows that humans expect a response within 200–500 ms of a turn ending; delays beyond 700 ms are perceived as hesitation or error.

Turn detection accuracy also directly affects transcription quality. Endpointing too early truncates the utterance, degrading ASR accuracy for the final words. Endpointing too late adds unnecessary silence frames that can confuse some ASR models and increases per-turn latency by the excess silence duration. In telephony and call-center applications, poor turn detection is the most common source of user complaints.
— correct endpointing matches the response latency humans experience in real dialogue (200–500 ms), making the agent feel responsive rather than sluggish.
— VAD operates on 10–30 ms frames incrementally, so it adds negligible latency to the pipeline and requires no buffering of the full utterance.
— Silero VAD processes one 30 ms chunk in under 2 ms on a single CPU core. WebRTC VAD runs in under 0.5 ms. Both are suitable for embedded and edge deployments.
— client-side VAD suppresses transmission of silence frames, reducing WebSocket or WebRTC bandwidth by 60–80% in typical conversations.
— ML-based VADs like Silero are language-agnostic; they detect phoneme-level activity without needing language-specific models or configuration.
— music, TV, air conditioning, and keyboard clicks can trigger speech onset. Energy-based VADs are especially prone to this; ML-based models reduce but do not eliminate it.
— speakers naturally pause mid-sentence (filled or empty pauses). A
set too low (< 400 ms) will cut off users during natural pauses; too high (> 800 ms) adds latency for every turn.
— VAD cannot distinguish between a complete sentence ("What time is it?") and an incomplete one that happens to end with silence ("I want to..."). Semantic detection adds latency but solves this.
— when the agent is speaking, the microphone may pick up its own TTS output, causing spurious turn detection. Requires acoustic echo cancellation (AEC) or agent-speaking gating.

— pause length norms vary by language and dialect. English speakers average 200 ms turn-final pauses; Mandarin speakers average 100 ms; Finish speakers allow longer silences in turn-medial positions. A single global threshold will be wrong for some user populations.

The simplest approach: compute the root-mean-square (RMS) energy of each audio frame and compare it to a static threshold. If energy exceeds the threshold, the frame is classified as speech. The main weakness is that the threshold must be tuned per microphone and environment; a phone ringing at the same amplitude as a quiet voice will trigger a false positive. Zero-crossing rate (how often the signal crosses zero amplitude) can be added as a second feature — speech has higher ZCR than most broadband noise, but lower ZCR than hiss.

Silero VAD is a 1.8 MB ONNX model trained on hundreds of languages. It accepts 16 kHz PCM audio in chunks of 512 samples (32 ms) and returns a speech probability between 0.0 and 1.0. The model is robust to background noise, microphone distortion, and multiple simultaneous speakers. It runs entirely on CPU in under 2 ms per chunk.

Python package or directly in browser WebRTC) uses Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) fit to spectral features. It operates on 10, 20, or 30 ms frames at 8, 16, 32, or 48 kHz and returns a binary speech/non-speech decision. An

parameter (0–3) trades false negatives for false positives: mode 0 is the least aggressive (few false positives, may miss soft speech); mode 3 is most aggressive (catches all speech, may also catch noise). Mode 2 is a good default for telephony environments.

A raw VAD output is noisy — individual frames misclassified as speech or non-speech. The turn detection layer smooths this with a state machine and two timers. When a configurable number of consecutive speech frames arrive, the detector transitions to the speech-active state and begins buffering audio (with
of pre-speech audio prepended to catch the utterance onset). When the speech probability drops below the threshold and the silence persists for
milliseconds, the detector commits the buffered audio as a completed turn. This prevents a single quiet frame from prematurely ending a turn.
The diagram below shows the VAD state machine alongside the audio energy waveform it is classifying. Each vertical bar represents one 32 ms audio frame. The dashed amber line is the
. Bars above the threshold trigger speech onset; sustained bars below the threshold for
trigger turn commit. The loop arrow at top shows the state machine resetting after each completed turn.

attrs.alt: VAD state machine: Silence → Speech Detected → Collecting Audio → End of Speech → Commit Turn → Process, with audio energy bar chart showing threshold crossing

content.text: when building a quick voice agent prototype on the OpenAI Realtime API, Deepgram, or similar. Zero client-side code; the provider handles the state machine.
when you need low latency, precise control over when turns are committed, or when your backend is not a hosted Realtime API (e.g., a custom ASR pipeline).
for multi-sentence or information-dense tasks (e.g., dictation, customer support scripting) where mid-sentence pauses are common and premature commit causes confusion.
for walkie-talkie-style interfaces, accessibility contexts (users with speech disorders often have atypical pause patterns), or high-noise industrial environments where VAD reliability is low.
— when the agent is speaking, automatic VAD will misfire on the agent's own TTS audio. Use manual turn control (
) and gate turn commits with an agent-speaking flag.
For the OpenAI Realtime API, enable server-side VAD in the session configuration. The API will automatically emit
events, then commit the buffer and (optionally) create a response automatically. The key session parameters are:
: speech probability cutoff (0.0–1.0). Default 0.5. Raise to 0.7–0.8 in noisy environments.
: how long silence must persist before the turn ends. Default 500 ms. Use 600–800 ms for deliberate speakers; 300–400 ms for quick back-and-forth interactions.
: audio prepended before speech onset (captures the first consonant that often precedes the threshold crossing). Default 300 ms.
, the API automatically creates an LLM response when the turn is committed. Set to
when you want to post-process the transcription before deciding whether to respond.
A stateful wrapper around the Silero model that processes 32 ms PCM chunks and emits turn events. Requires

import torch
import numpy as np

# Downloads ~1.8 MB ONNX model on first call; cached afterward
model, utils = torch.hub.load(
    repo_or_dir='snakers4/silero-vad',
    model='silero_vad',
    force_reload=False,
)

SAMPLE_RATE = 16000
CHUNK_SAMPLES = 512   # 32 ms at 16 kHz (valid sizes: 256, 512, 768)


class SileroTurnDetector:
    """Stateful turn detector for streaming 16 kHz PCM audio."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        threshold: float = 0.5,
        silence_duration_ms: int = 500,
        prefix_padding_ms: int = 300,
    ):
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.silence_frames_needed = int(
            silence_duration_ms / (CHUNK_SAMPLES / SAMPLE_RATE * 1000)
        )
        self.prefix_frame_count = int(
            prefix_padding_ms / (CHUNK_SAMPLES / SAMPLE_RATE * 1000)
        )
        self._in_speech = False
        self._silence_count = 0
        self._audio_buffer: list[np.ndarray] = []
        self._prefix_ring: list[np.ndarray] = []

    def process_chunk(self, pcm_chunk: np.ndarray) -> dict | None:
        """
        Process one 32 ms PCM chunk (float32, range -1..1, 16 kHz).
        Returns a turn event dict or None.

        Events:
            {'event': 'speech_start'}
            {'event': 'speech_end', 'audio': np.ndarray}  # full utterance
        """
        speech_prob = model(torch.FloatTensor(pcm_chunk), SAMPLE_RATE).item()

        # Maintain rolling prefix buffer
        self._prefix_ring.append(pcm_chunk)
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Example 2 — WebRTC VAD with ring buffer
WebRTC VAD uses GMM-based classification and is available natively in browser audio pipelines and via
on the server. This implementation uses a ring buffer to smooth the binary speech/non-speech signal.

import webrtcvad
import collections
import numpy as np


class WebRtcTurnDetector:
    """
    Turn detector using WebRTC's GMM-based VAD.
    Audio must be 16-bit PCM (raw bytes), 16 kHz, 30 ms frames.
    pip install webrtcvad
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        aggressiveness: int = 2,       # 0 = least, 3 = most aggressive
        sample_rate: int = 16000,
        frame_duration_ms: int = 30,   # must be 10, 20, or 30
        silence_duration_ms: int = 500,
        speech_trigger_ratio: float = 0.9,
    ):
        self.vad = webrtcvad.Vad(aggressiveness)
        self.sample_rate = sample_rate
        self.frame_duration_ms = frame_duration_ms
        # Bytes per frame: samples * 2 bytes (int16)
        self.frame_bytes = int(sample_rate * frame_duration_ms / 1000) * 2

        num_silence_frames = silence_duration_ms // frame_duration_ms
        self._ring: collections.deque[bool] = collections.deque(
            maxlen=num_silence_frames
        )
        self._triggered = False
        self._voiced_frames: list[bytes] = []
        self._trigger_ratio = speech_trigger_ratio

    def process_frame(self, raw_bytes: bytes) -> dict | None:
        """
        Process one PCM frame (int16 little-endian, raw bytes).
        Returns turn event dict or None.
        """
        assert len(raw_bytes) == self.frame_bytes, (
            f'Expected {self.frame_bytes} bytes, got {len(raw_bytes)}'
        )
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Example 3 — OpenAI Realtime API server_vad configuration

import asyncio
import json
import base64
import websockets

OPENAI_RT_URL = 'wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?model=gpt-4o-realtime-preview'


async def voice_agent(api_key: str, audio_stream) -> None:
    """Connect to OpenAI Realtime API with server-side VAD."""
    headers = {
        'Authorization': f'Bearer {api_key}',
        'OpenAI-Beta': 'realtime=v1',
    }
    async with websockets.connect(OPENAI_RT_URL, additional_headers=headers) as ws:
        # Configure session: enable server_vad turn detection
        await ws.send(json.dumps({
            'type': 'session.update',
            'session': {
                'modalities': ['text', 'audio'],
                'voice': 'alloy',
                'input_audio_format': 'pcm16',
                'output_audio_format': 'pcm16',
                'input_audio_transcription': {'model': 'whisper-1'},
                'turn_detection': {
                    'type': 'server_vad',
                    'threshold': 0.5,             # speech prob cutoff (0.0–1.0)
                    'prefix_padding_ms': 300,      # audio prepended to utterance
                    'silence_duration_ms': 500,    # silence needed to end turn
                    'create_response': True,       # auto-create LLM reply
                },
                'instructions': 'You are a helpful voice assistant. Be brief.',
            },
        }))

        async def send_loop():
            async for chunk in audio_stream:   # raw 16-bit PCM bytes
                await ws.send(json.dumps({
                    'type': 'input_audio_buffer.append',
                    'audio': base64.b64encode(chunk).decode(),
                }))

        async def recv_loop():
            async for raw in ws:
                event = json.loads(raw)
                etype = event.get('type', '')

                if etype == 'input_audio_buffer.speech_started':
                    print(f"[VAD] Speech onset detected")

                elif etype == 'input_audio_buffer.speech_stopped':
                    print(f"[VAD] End of speech — committing turn")

                elif etype == 'input_audio_buffer.committed':
                    print(f"[VAD] Turn committed: {event.get('item_id')}")

                elif etype == 'conversation.item.input_audio_transcription.completed':
                    print(f"[ASR] '{event['transcript']}'")

                elif etype == 'response.audio.delta':
                    audio = base64.b64decode(event['delta'])
                    # play_audio_chunk(audio)

                elif etype == 'response.done':
                    print('[Agent] Response complete')

                elif etype == 'error':
                    print(f"[Error] {event['error']}")

        await asyncio.gather(send_loop(), recv_loop())

In production voice agents, server_vad often fires spuriously when the agent is speaking — the microphone captures the agent's own TTS audio and triggers a false turn. The fix is a hybrid approach: use client-side VAD to gate when turns are committed to the server, with the client suppressing commits while the agent is speaking. This eliminates echo-triggered false positives without requiring acoustic echo cancellation on every device.

import asyncio
import json
import base64
import websockets
import torch
import numpy as np

# Reuse the SileroTurnDetector from Example 1

OPENAI_RT_URL = 'wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?model=gpt-4o-realtime-preview'


async def hybrid_voice_agent(api_key: str, audio_stream) -> None:
    """
    Hybrid approach:
    - server_vad disabled (turn_detection=None)
    - client-side Silero VAD determines turn boundaries
    - commits are suppressed while agent is speaking
    """
    headers = {
        'Authorization': f'Bearer {api_key}',
        'OpenAI-Beta': 'realtime=v1',
    }
    async with websockets.connect(OPENAI_RT_URL, additional_headers=headers) as ws:
        # Disable server-side VAD — we manage turns manually
        await ws.send(json.dumps({
            'type': 'session.update',
            'session': {
                'modalities': ['text', 'audio'],
                'turn_detection': None,   # manual control
            },
        }))

        agent_speaking = False

        async def recv_loop():
            nonlocal agent_speaking
            async for raw in ws:
                ev = json.loads(raw)
                if ev['type'] == 'response.audio.delta':
                    agent_speaking = True
                elif ev['type'] in ('response.done', 'response.audio.done'):
                    agent_speaking = False

        asyncio.create_task(recv_loop())

        detector = SileroTurnDetector(threshold=0.5, silence_duration_ms=600)

        async for pcm_chunk in audio_stream:  # float32 np array, 16 kHz
            # Always append audio so the server has full context
            await ws.send(json.dumps({
                'type': 'input_audio_buffer.append',
                'audio': base64.b64encode(
                    (pcm_chunk * 32767).astype(np.int16).tobytes()
                ).decode(),
            }))

            event = detector.process_chunk(pcm_chunk)
            if event and event['event'] == 'speech_end' and not agent_speaking:
                # Manually commit the turn and request a response
                await ws.send(json.dumps({'type': 'input_audio_buffer.commit'}))
                await ws.send(json.dumps({'type': 'response.create'}))
                print('Turn committed to server')
            elif event and event['event'] == 'speech_end' and agent_speaking:
                # Agent was speaking — discard this turn to avoid echo response
                await ws.send(json.dumps({'type': 'input_audio_buffer.clear'}))
                print('Turn discarded (agent was speaking)')

The most common mistake. A value of 200–300 ms will cut off users during natural clause-final pauses ("I want to order... a large coffee"). The user experience is that the agent constantly interrupts. Natural pause durations in English are typically 200–400 ms within sentences and 600–800 ms at sentence boundaries. Start at 500 ms and lower only after measuring actual miss rates on real traffic.
Speech onset is detected after the energy threshold is crossed, but the actual first phoneme (often a voiceless stop like /t/ or /p/) begins slightly before the threshold crossing. Without
, the ASR model receives audio that starts mid-phoneme. This reliably degrades word-error-rate for utterances that begin with consonants. The OpenAI default of 300 ms is a good starting point.

When using server_vad without acoustic echo cancellation, the microphone captures the agent's TTS output. The VAD interprets this as a new user utterance and triggers a spurious turn commit, causing the agent to respond to its own voice. Always track agent speaking state and either suppress audio buffer appends or clear the buffer when the agent stops speaking.
Using the wrong audio format with WebRTC VAD

WebRTC VAD requires exactly 10, 20, or 30 ms frames of 16-bit signed PCM. Passing float32 arrays, mono-vs-stereo mismatches, or incorrect sample rates will raise silent wrong-classification bugs or hard exceptions. Always assert the frame length before calling

— log every turn commit with its duration and whether the user barged in immediately. Track false positive rate (turn ended mid-utterance) and false negative rate (excessive latency). Use these metrics to drive threshold changes, not intuition.
Layer VAD with acoustic echo cancellation
— browser WebRTC tracks and Web Audio API both offer built-in AEC. Enable it to prevent agent TTS audio from triggering spurious turns, reducing the need for the manual-gate workaround.
Validate on your target accent and language
needs per-language calibration. Mandarin speakers use shorter inter-utterance pauses than English speakers.
Use hysteresis in the state machine

— require multiple consecutive speech frames to trigger onset (avoid single-frame noise spikes) and multiple silence frames to trigger offset (avoid cutting off during natural pauses). The ring buffer approach in Example 2 implements this.
— reject turns shorter than 300 ms. Coughs, clicks, and non-speech vocalizations will trigger VAD but produce meaningless ASR output. Filtering short turns avoids wasted LLM calls.

— short back-channels ("mm-hmm", "yeah", "ok") should not interrupt the agent. Either filter turns shorter than 700 ms or run a lightweight intent classifier to distinguish back-channels from full turns.

Silero VAD processes each 32 ms chunk in approximately 1–2 ms on a single CPU core (ONNX runtime). At 16 kHz the VAD must process roughly 31 chunks per second, requiring about 50–60 ms of CPU time per second — less than 6% CPU load on a single core. Memory footprint is 50–80 MB (model weights + runtime). Because the model is stateless per-chunk, it scales horizontally by simply running one instance per concurrent stream.
WebRTC VAD is faster: under 0.5 ms per 30 ms frame, negligible memory. It is the right choice for edge deployments (mobile, embedded) and browser-side JavaScript (via the

ONNX WASM package, which bundles Silero and runs in the browser at comparable latency). Doing VAD client-side also reduces server load and bandwidth: only speech frames are transmitted, cutting audio upload volume by 60–80% in typical conversations.

At scale (thousands of concurrent streams), client-side VAD is strongly preferred because it shifts the computation to users' devices. If server-side VAD is required (e.g., telephony where the client is a dumb phone), use a dedicated VAD service rather than running it in the main agent process, as the continuous audio stream requires sustained CPU allocation unrelated to LLM call volume.
Best accuracy; 100+ languages; 1.8 MB model; Python/JS WASM
Built into browser WebRTC; 4 aggressiveness modes; Python via C extension
Client-side JS/TS; emits speech start/end events; React hook available
Zero client code; emits speech_started / speech_stopped events; configurable threshold/silence_duration_ms
Combines ASR and VAD; emits SpeechFinal=true on utterance end; tunable via endpointing param
Python voice agent framework with built-in VAD pipeline and barge-in support
Rapid prototypes on OpenAI Realtime API
Production agents where both noise robustness and semantic accuracy matter

— adversaries can play ultrasonic audio (18–22 kHz) inaudible to humans that some microphones and VAD models interpret as speech. ML-based VADs trained on narrowband audio are more susceptible. Enforce a bandpass filter (300 Hz–8 kHz) before VAD processing and set a maximum turn length.

— a client sending rapid consecutive short turns (sub-200 ms gaps) is either generating noise or performing a denial-of-service attack. Enforce a minimum inter-turn interval of 200–300 ms server-side.
— without a ceiling on buffered audio, a stuck microphone or continuous noise source will fill memory indefinitely. Commit and discard the buffer after 30–60 seconds.
— malformed audio (wrong sample rate, corrupted frames) can cause silent VAD misclassification or crashes in native extensions like
. Validate frame length and sample rate before calling the VAD.
Log and alert on VAD anomalies

— a sudden spike in turns-per-minute, very short turn durations, or high false positive rates all indicate environmental changes (device moved, noisy location) or adversarial inputs. Surface these as metrics for operations teams.
Turn detection wraps a VAD in a state machine: collect frames above
, then commit when silence persists for
. Tuning these two parameters accounts for 80% of the work.
Silero VAD (ML, 1.8 MB ONNX) is the go-to choice for production: robust to noise, 100+ language coverage, 1–2 ms per 32 ms chunk on CPU, runs client-side in WASM.
mode is the fastest path to a working voice agent, but requires disabling or gating when the agent is speaking to prevent echo-triggered false turns.
(default 300 ms) — without it, the first phoneme of each utterance is clipped, reliably raising word-error-rate.
Client-side VAD reduces server load and audio bandwidth by 60–80%; at scale it is the correct architecture to avoid running continuous audio processing in the main agent process.

Semantic turn detection (running a small LLM classifier on the partial transcript) is the right layer to add when users exhibit frequent mid-sentence pauses; it adds 200–500 ms latency but eliminates premature commits.
Measure false positive and false negative rates on real traffic before changing thresholds — intuition about "it feels too slow" is often solved by
adjustment; "it interrupts me" is solved by raising
